bibliographic_data.title: Large Language Models for JSON Schema Discovery
bibliographic_data.abstract: Semi-structured data formats such as JSON have proved to be useful data models for applications that require flexibility in the format of data stored. However, JSON data often come without the schemas that are typically available with relational data. This has resulted in a number of tools for discovering schemas from a collection of data. Although such tools can be useful, existing approaches focus on the syntax of documents and ignore semantic information. In this work, we explore the automatic addition of meaningful semantic information to discovered schemas similar to information that is added by human schema authors. We leverage large language models and a corpus of manually authored JSON Schema documents to generate natural language descriptions of schema elements, meaningful names for reusable definitions, and identify which discovered properties are most useful and which can be considered “noise”. Our approach performs well on existing metrics for text generation that have been previously shown to correlate well with human judgement.

core_content.novelty_claim: The work explores the use of large language models to add meaningful semantic information to automatically discovered JSON schemas, similar to what is typically added by human schema authors.
core_content.key_methodology: Leveraging a corpus of manually authored JSON Schema documents, the approach generates natural language descriptions for schema elements and provides semantically meaningful names for reusable definitions. It also builds a classifier to identify useful properties from automatically discovered schemas.
outcomes: Generated natural language descriptions for schema elements
Provided semantically meaningful names for reusable definitions
Built a classifier to identify useful properties in automatically discovered schemas
core_content.full_text_markdown: Large language models (LLMs) such as ChatGPT have had a significant impact across all fields of computer science. One common application is natural language question answering, but LLMs have also been used for software development tasks like intelligent autocompletion and even developing new applications from scratch. In this work, the authors explore several applications of LLMs in schema discovery for semi-structured data formats like JSON. They use properties discovered from datasets to provide natural language descriptions of elements of a schema, demonstrate how definitions of repeated subschemas can be given semantically meaningful names, and build a classifier to identify useful properties in automatically discovered schemas. The results are validated using a dataset of several hundred real-world JSON Schemas.
